perturbation.original_text: 3. Timing of Required Capital Contributions. The Required Capital Contributions shall be made within forty-five (45) days following the last day of each Fiscal Quarter, unless the last day of such Fiscal Quarter is the same as the fiscal year end, and in such case, ninety (90) days following such fiscal year end.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Timing of Required Capital Contributions. The Required Capital Contributions shall be made within one hundred and eighty (180) days following the last day of each Fiscal Quarter, regardless of whether the last day of such Fiscal Quarter is the same as the fiscal year end.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment timeline to 180 days after the quarter ends creates an unreasonably long delay, which might violate standard business practices and potentially breach implied duties of good faith and fair dealing.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Minnesota Statutes § 336.1-304 Obligation of good faith
perturbation.law_explanation: This statute states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Setting an unreasonably long period for capital contributions could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, especially if it harms the lender's ability to manage its finances or increases their risk.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 336.1-304 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes TRADE REGULATIONS, CONSUMER PROTECTION Chapter 336 Section 336.1-304 336.1-303 336.1-305 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 336 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 336.1-304 Version List Recent History 2004 336.1-304 New 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 336.1-304 OBLIGATION OF GOOD FAITH. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. History: 2004 c 162 art 1 s 19 Official Publication of the State of Minnesota Revisor of Statutes
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment timeline to 180 days, regardless of the fiscal year end. The Law Explanation suggests this unreasonably long period could be interpreted as acting in bad faith, harming the lender's ability to manage finances or increasing their risk. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that every contract within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance. By extending the payment timeline substantially, the changed text contradicts the obligation of good faith, creating an inconsistency as defined in the provided perturbation definitions, thus making the terms unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: 15. GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY, CONSTRUCTION AND ENFORCEABILITY OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF MINNESOTA, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO CONFLICT OF LAWS PRINCIPLES THEREOF.
perturbation.changed_text: 15. GOVERNING LAW. THE VALIDITY, CONSTRUCTION AND ENFORCEABILITY OF THIS AGREEMENT SHALL BE GOVERNED BY THE LAWS OF THE STATE OF DELAWARE, WITHOUT GIVING EFFECT TO CONFLICT OF LAWS PRINCIPLES THEREOF.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Minnesota to Delaware could affect the enforceability of certain clauses, especially if Delaware law is less favorable to certain provisions within the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: Switching to Delaware law could impact the statute of limitations for breach of contract claims. Minnesota has a six-year statute of limitations for contract actions. If Delaware has a shorter limitations period, this change could prematurely bar legal claims that would have been timely under Minnesota law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Sec. 541.05 MN Statutes Skip to main content Skip to office menu Skip to footer Minnesota Legislature Advanced Search Search Legislature Search Office of the Revisor of Statutes Office of the Revisor of Statutes 2024 Minnesota Statutes CIVIL PROCEDURE Chapter 541 Section 541.05 541.04 541.051 2024 Minnesota Statutes Authenticate PDF Resources Search Minnesota Statutes About Minnesota Statutes 2024 Statutes New, Amended or Repealed 2024 Table of Chapters 2024 Statutes Topics (Index) Chapter 541 Table of Sections Full Chapter Text Version List Section 541.05 Version List Recent History 2015 Subd. 1 Amended 2015 c 5 art 15 s 18 2000 Subd. 1 Amended 2000 c 471 s 1 541.05 VARIOUS CASES, SIX YEARS. § Subdivision 1. Six-year limitation. Except where the Uniform Commercial Code otherwise prescribes, the following actions shall be commenced within six years: (1) upon a contract or other obligation, express or implied, as to which no other limitation is expressly prescribed; (2) upon a liability created by statute, other than those arising upon a penalty or forfeiture or where a shorter period is provided by section 541.07 ; (3) for a trespass upon real estate; (4) for taking, detaining, or injuring personal property, including actions for the specific recovery thereof; (5) for criminal conversation, or for any other injury to the person or rights of another, not arising on contract, and not hereinafter enumerated; (6) for relief on the ground of fraud, in which case the cause of action shall not be deemed to have accrued until the discovery by the aggrieved party of the facts constituting the fraud; (7) against sureties upon the official bond of any public officer, whether of the state or of any county, town, school district, or a municipality therein; in which case the limitation shall not begin to run until the term of such officer for which the bond was given shall have expired; (8) for damages caused by a dam, used for commercial purposes; or (9) for assault, battery, false impriso
perturbation.justification: The change in governing law from Minnesota to Delaware introduces a legal contradiction, specifically concerning the statute of limitations for contract breach claims. The LLM's law explanation points out that Minnesota has a six-year statute of limitations. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly states in Section 541.05 Subd. 1 that actions upon a contract shall be commenced within six years unless otherwise prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code. If Delaware has a shorter statute of limitations, as suggested by the LLM's explanation, then the change could prematurely bar legal claims that would be timely under Minnesota law. This creates a contradiction because the enforceability and timeline for legal action are altered, potentially diminishing the protection afforded under the original governing law.
